findings.title: JWT signing key uses a static shared secret with no JWKS endpoint
findings.description: The plan defines JWT validation across all services using a single RSA key pair stored as a ConfigMap (public key) and ExternalSecret (private key). There is no OIDC/JWKS endpoint for key discovery, no key ID (kid) header support, and no mechanism for key rotation without simultaneously restarting all services. The shared-auth crate (subtask 2004) reads JWT_SECRET from an env var, suggesting some services may use HMAC-SHA256 while others use RSA-256 (task 10001), creating an inconsistency. If the single signing key is compromised, all service-to-service and user-to-service authentication is broken with no revocation mechanism.
findings.recommendation: Implement a JWKS endpoint (even a static file served via a lightweight service or ConfigMap-mounted JSON) with key ID (kid) support. All services should validate tokens by fetching the JWKS and matching the kid. Standardize on RS256 across all stacks. Support multiple active keys during rotation (old key + new key both valid). Add a token revocation list in Valkey for emergency revocation of compromised tokens.

findings.title: No user authentication system defined — JWT issuance for end users is unspecified
findings.description: The plan defines service-to-service JWT tokens (task 10002) and JWT validation middleware in every service, but there is no task for user authentication: no login flow, no identity provider integration, no user registration, no session management for human users. The RBAC ConfigMap defines roles (admin, operator, morgan-agent, readonly) but there is no mechanism to authenticate human users and assign them these roles. The frontend (task 8) has no login page or auth flow. This means either all API endpoints are effectively public (only protected by service tokens), or there is an assumed external IdP that is not documented.
findings.recommendation: Add a task to integrate an identity provider (e.g., Clerk, Auth0, Keycloak, or Cloudflare Access with JWT minting) for human user authentication. Define the user login flow, token issuance, role assignment, and how user JWTs differ from service JWTs. The frontend needs a login/auth page or redirect flow. All service auth middleware needs to handle both user JWTs and service JWTs.

findings.title: Database credentials stored in plain text in ConfigMap
findings.description: Subtask 1007 (sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap) acknowledges that passwords should NOT be in the ConfigMap but then defines per-service DATABASE_URL entries with '<secret-ref>' placeholders. The consumption pattern (init container constructing full URLs, or services mounting both ConfigMap and Secret) is described as 'document the intended consumption pattern' rather than implemented. This leaves a high risk that credentials end up in ConfigMaps, environment variables visible in pod specs, or application logs.
findings.recommendation: Implement a concrete credential injection pattern: (1) Store only hostnames/ports in the ConfigMap, (2) Store credentials in separate Kubernetes Secrets per service user, (3) Use init containers or sidecar (like vault-agent) to construct full connection URLs at runtime and write them to a shared emptyDir volume. Add explicit validation in the test strategy that no Secret data appears in ConfigMap values.

findings.title: Stripe webhook endpoint lacks authentication middleware but needs raw body access
findings.description: Subtask 4006 correctly identifies that the Stripe webhook endpoint must be excluded from the standard auth middleware and use Stripe signature verification instead. However, the implementation detail says 'Register webhook route WITHOUT auth middleware' which creates an unauthenticated endpoint. If the Stripe webhook signing secret is not properly validated, or if the raw body extraction fails, this endpoint becomes an open attack vector for forged payment events that could manipulate invoice statuses and record fraudulent payments.
findings.recommendation: Ensure the Stripe webhook handler: (1) Always validates the Stripe-Signature header before processing any event, (2) Returns 400 immediately on invalid signatures, (3) Uses a custom Axum extractor that captures raw bytes before any deserialization, (4) Rate limits the webhook endpoint separately (e.g., 50 req/min), (5) Logs all webhook events including rejected ones to the audit log. Add an integration test that sends a webhook with an invalid signature and verifies rejection.

findings.title: SQL injection risk in dynamic query builders across multiple services
findings.description: Multiple services build dynamic SQL queries: Equipment Catalog (subtask 2008) builds dynamic queries with sqlx for product filtering, RMS (subtask 3005) uses pgx for repository queries, and Finance (subtask 4003) builds dynamic UPDATE SET queries for partial updates. While sqlx and pgx use parameterized queries by default, the dynamic query building patterns (conditional WHERE clauses, dynamic SET clauses) are error-prone. No explicit validation of filter parameters against allowed values is mentioned.
findings.recommendation: For all dynamic query builders: (1) Use sqlx/pgx query builder patterns that guarantee parameterization — never concatenate user input into SQL strings, (2) Validate enum-type filter values (status, category) against a whitelist before including in queries, (3) Validate sort column names against an allowlist (prevent ORDER BY injection), (4) Add Semgrep custom rules specifically for Rust sqlx and Go pgx to detect raw SQL string concatenation patterns. Include SQL injection test cases in each service's test strategy.

findings.title: PII in audit logs and structured logging
findings.description: Structured logging is implemented across all services (zerolog in Go, tracing in Rust, prom-client in Node.js) with request details logged at info level. The audit logging system (task 10005) stores actor_user_id, entity_id, and request_metadata (IP, user-agent). However, there is no data classification or PII scrubbing policy. Service logs may contain customer names, email addresses, phone numbers, or financial data in request/response bodies. The MCP tool server (subtask 7007) logs input_params with 'redacted sensitive fields' mentioned but not specified.
findings.recommendation: Define a PII classification policy and implement log scrubbing: (1) Create a shared log sanitizer that redacts email addresses, phone numbers, credit card patterns, and names from log output, (2) Define which fields in audit_log.request_metadata are permitted (allowlist, not blocklist), (3) For the MCP tool server, explicitly list which input fields are redacted (e.g., customer_email, credit card info), (4) Configure log retention policies aligned with GDPR (e.g., 90 days for operational logs, 7 years for audit logs).

findings.title: Signal-CLI sidecar uses unpinned latest tag from third-party registry
findings.description: Subtask 7008 specifies the Signal-CLI sidecar image as `bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api:latest`. This is a third-party Docker Hub image with a mutable 'latest' tag. The image could be compromised via a supply chain attack on the maintainer's Docker Hub account, or could break due to unexpected updates. The sidecar has access to the Morgan pod's network namespace and can communicate with all backend services via localhost.
findings.recommendation: Pin the signal-cli-rest-api image to a specific SHA digest (e.g., `bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api@sha256:...`). Set imagePullPolicy to IfNotPresent. Run the image through a container scanner (Trivy/Snyk) before deploying. Consider building a custom signal-cli container from source to eliminate the third-party trust dependency. Add the image to the Kyverno/Gatekeeper allowed registries policy (task 10009).

findings.title: Dependency versions not pinned across all stacks
findings.description: Multiple task descriptions use version ranges rather than pinned versions: Elysia `@^1.0`, Effect `@^3.0`, TypeScript `@^5.0` (subtask 6001). The Rust workspace uses workspace-level dependencies but doesn't mention Cargo.lock commitment. Go modules reference packages without explicit version pinning in the task descriptions. Unpinned dependencies can introduce vulnerabilities through automatic minor/patch updates.
findings.recommendation: Commit lock files to version control: Cargo.lock (Rust), go.sum (Go), bun.lockb (Bun/Node.js). In CI, use `--frozen-lockfile` (bun) or equivalent to prevent lock file drift. Pin major+minor versions in package.json/Cargo.toml (e.g., `~1.0.x` not `^1.0`). Add the Dependabot/Snyk configuration from task 10010 as a high-priority prerequisite before any service goes to production.

findings.title: File upload endpoint lacks comprehensive security controls
findings.description: The social media upload endpoint (subtask 6004) accepts multipart/form-data with 'multiple image files' and performs only a MIME type check. There is no file size limit, no image dimension validation, no content scanning for embedded malware, no check for polyglot files (files that are valid as both images and scripts), and no limit on the number of files per upload. Uploaded files are stored directly in R2 and served via CDN, creating a potential vector for stored XSS via SVG files or malware distribution.
findings.recommendation: Add comprehensive upload validation: (1) Maximum file size (e.g., 50MB per file), (2) Maximum files per request (e.g., 20), (3) Content-type validation by reading magic bytes (not just the MIME header), (4) Reject SVG files (common XSS vector) or sanitize them, (5) Strip EXIF data containing GPS coordinates and PII before storing, (6) Set Content-Disposition: attachment on non-image content types in R2, (7) Configure R2/CDN to serve images with Content-Type: image/* only and add Content-Security-Policy headers to prevent script execution.

findings.title: RBAC defined as a ConfigMap with no enforcement mechanism
findings.description: The RBAC role definitions (subtask 1008) are stored as a ConfigMap JSON file. Each service is expected to 'implement permission checks against this matrix' independently. This shared-nothing RBAC approach means: (1) each service may implement the checks differently or incorrectly, (2) role changes require ConfigMap update + restart of all services, (3) there is no centralized audit of role enforcement, (4) the ConfigMap is world-readable within the namespace. The morgan-agent role has broad read access to all services which could be exploited if Morgan's token is compromised.
findings.recommendation: Create a shared RBAC library/crate that all services import, ensuring consistent enforcement. Add integration tests per service that verify role-based access control for each endpoint. Consider using OPA/Cedar for centralized policy evaluation. For the morgan-agent role, implement additional controls: rate-limit Morgan's API calls, log all tool invocations to the audit log, and add an alert for unusual access patterns (e.g., Morgan accessing vetting data at abnormal rates).

findings.title: Valkey (Redis) deployed without authentication
findings.description: The Valkey instance (subtask 1004) is deployed with no authentication configured — no requirepass, no ACLs. Any pod that can reach Valkey on port 6379 can read/write all data. While Cilium network policies (subtask 1009) restrict access to sigma1 namespace pods, if any pod in the namespace is compromised, the attacker has full access to cached data, session tokens, conversation history, rate limiting state, and cached vetting results.
findings.recommendation: Enable Valkey authentication: (1) Set a strong password via requirepass (generate and store as ExternalSecret), (2) Configure ACLs if supported by Opstree operator — create per-service users with limited command sets (e.g., social engine only needs GET/SET/DEL, not KEYS/FLUSHALL), (3) Update all service connection strings to include authentication credentials, (4) Disable dangerous commands: FLUSHALL, FLUSHDB, KEYS, CONFIG in production.

findings.title: PostgreSQL connections not confirmed to use TLS
findings.description: The CloudNative-PG cluster setup (subtasks 1002, 1003) and connection strings in the ConfigMap (subtask 1007) do not explicitly mention TLS for database connections. While CNPG supports TLS by default, the connection strings don't include `sslmode=require` or `sslmode=verify-full`. Without TLS enforcement, database traffic within the cluster is transmitted in plain text, making it vulnerable to network sniffing if a pod is compromised.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly configure TLS in CNPG: (1) Verify CNPG cluster CR has TLS enabled (default in modern CNPG versions), (2) Add `sslmode=require` or `sslmode=verify-full` to all database connection strings in the ConfigMap, (3) Distribute the CNPG CA certificate to services and configure their connection pools to verify it, (4) Add a test in the validation suite (subtask 1011) that confirms connections without TLS are rejected.

findings.title: No security event alerting or anomaly detection
findings.description: The monitoring setup (task 9, subtask 9012) focuses on operational metrics (pod health, latency, resource usage) but lacks security-specific alerting. There are no alerts for: failed authentication attempts, privilege escalation attempts (403 spikes), unusual API access patterns, audit log anomalies, database permission denied events, or network policy violations. The AlertManager rules (subtask 10012) cover service availability but not security events.
findings.recommendation: Add security-focused AlertManager rules: (1) Alert on >50 HTTP 401 responses in 5 minutes (brute force attempt), (2) Alert on >20 HTTP 403 responses from a single service identity in 5 minutes (potential token misuse), (3) Alert on Cilium network policy deny events from unexpected source pods, (4) Alert on any SELECT/DELETE on audit schema (should be INSERT only), (5) Alert on failed database authentication attempts, (6) Create a Grafana security dashboard showing auth failures, RBAC denials, and network policy violations over time.

findings.title: GDPR data export uses R2 signed URLs with 7-day expiry without access controls
findings.description: The GDPR data export Job (subtask 10006) uploads customer data to R2 and generates a signed URL with 7-day expiry. This URL is stored in the database. Anyone with the URL can access the full customer data export. There is no additional access control (e.g., requiring authentication), no download audit logging, and no mechanism to revoke the URL before expiry if needed. The export contains aggregated data from ALL services for a customer, making it a high-value target.
findings.recommendation: Add access controls to GDPR exports: (1) Instead of a direct R2 signed URL, serve exports through an authenticated API endpoint that verifies the requester is authorized (admin role) and logs the download, (2) Use shorter signed URL expiry (24-48 hours), (3) Encrypt the export file at rest with a customer-specific key, (4) Add a mechanism to revoke/invalidate export URLs, (5) Log every access to the export URL in the audit log, (6) Send a notification when an export is downloaded.

findings.title: WebSocket chat endpoint authentication is ambiguous
findings.description: The Morgan web chat WebSocket endpoint (subtask 7011) uses session tokens stored in localStorage for session continuity. However, the authentication model is unclear: the session token is self-generated by the client (a UUID), not issued by an authentication service. This means anyone can create a new session and chat with Morgan without authentication. While this may be intentional for public-facing chat, Morgan has access to 11 MCP tools that can create invoices, run vetting, generate quotes, and access financial reports. The sales-qual workflow (subtask 7013) collects customer PII (name, email, phone) through an unauthenticated channel.
findings.recommendation: Define explicit authentication tiers for Morgan: (1) Unauthenticated web chat should only allow general inquiries and equipment browsing (catalog_search, check_availability), (2) Actions that create records (generate_quote, create_invoice, vet_customer) should require the user to provide verifiable contact info that Morgan validates, (3) MCP tools that access financial data (finance_report) should require authenticated admin sessions only, (4) Implement session abuse detection: rate limit message frequency, detect PII extraction attempts, and flag suspicious conversation patterns.

findings.title: Morgan PVC shared between agent container and Signal-CLI sidecar
findings.description: Subtask 7015 describes a single PVC `morgan-workspace-pvc` (10Gi) shared between the Morgan agent container and the Signal-CLI sidecar via subPath mounts. The Signal-CLI stores sensitive device registration keys and message history on this volume. If the Morgan container is compromised, the attacker could access Signal device keys and impersonate Morgan's Signal identity or read message history.
findings.recommendation: Use separate PVCs for the Morgan agent workspace and Signal-CLI data. Apply appropriate volume security: (1) Mount Signal-CLI data as read-only in the Morgan container if it only needs to read configuration, (2) Use a separate PVC for Signal-CLI device data with restricted ownership, (3) Ensure Signal-CLI data directory permissions are 0700 owned by the signal-cli user.

findings.title: Rate limiting can be bypassed via distributed IP addresses
findings.description: Rate limiting is implemented at two levels: per-service in Valkey (subtask 2010) and at the Cloudflare WAF level (subtask 10011). Both use per-IP rate limiting. Sophisticated attackers can distribute requests across many IPs to bypass per-IP limits. The Valkey-based rate limiter uses IP when no JWT is present, and JWT subject when authenticated. There is no account-level rate limiting for authenticated users across multiple services.
findings.recommendation: Layer rate limiting: (1) Keep per-IP limits at the Cloudflare WAF level as the first defense, (2) Add per-account rate limiting for authenticated users in Valkey (aggregate across all services), (3) Implement a global request budget per session token for the Morgan chat endpoint, (4) Consider Cloudflare's Bot Management features for detecting distributed attacks, (5) Add rate limiting metrics to the security Grafana dashboard for visibility.

findings.title: OpenAI Vision API receives customer event images for curation scoring
findings.description: The ImageCurationService (subtask 6005) sends uploaded event photos to OpenAI Vision API for scoring. These photos may contain identifiable individuals (event attendees), private venues, or other sensitive content. The data processing agreement with OpenAI and implications for GDPR consent (if EU individuals are photographed) are not addressed.
findings.recommendation: Document the data processing flow in the privacy policy: (1) Inform uploaders that images will be processed by AI services, (2) Verify OpenAI's data processing terms are compatible with your GDPR obligations (OpenAI's API data is not used for training by default, but confirm), (3) Consider face-blurring detection before sending to external APIs, (4) Add a consent checkbox in the upload flow, (5) Include AI processing in the GDPR data export (list which images were sent to which AI service).

strengths: Excellent database schema isolation via per-service PostgreSQL users with schema-scoped permissions preventing cross-schema JOINs — this is a strong data boundary enforcement mechanism
Comprehensive Cilium NetworkPolicy design with default-deny ingress, namespace isolation, and fine-grained per-service allow rules including separate ingress and egress policies

GDPR compliance is explicitly addressed with dedicated audit logging schema, data export orchestration, data deletion orchestration with per-service tracking, and the customer vetting service includes a DELETE endpoint for right-to-erasure
Multi-stage Docker builds using distroless base images across all services significantly reduces the container attack surface
Pod Security Standards enforcement at the 'restricted' level with runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, and capability dropping is properly planned as a production hardening task
ExternalSecrets operator usage for all third-party API keys keeps credentials out of Git and enables centralized secret lifecycle management
Stripe webhook integration correctly identifies the need for raw body extraction and signature verification as a security-critical implementation detail
Service-to-service JWT tokens with 90-day expiry and automated rotation CronJob demonstrate good credential lifecycle management planning
CI/CD security pipeline with Semgrep, Snyk/Dependabot, and CodeQL covering all three language stacks (Rust, Go, TypeScript) with merge blockers for critical findings
summary: The plan demonstrates strong infrastructure security fundamentals — database schema isolation, network policies, pod security standards, and GDPR compliance are well-architected. However, there are critical gaps in the authentication layer: no user identity provider is defined, JWT key management lacks JWKS support, and the Morgan AI agent's chat endpoint has unclear authentication boundaries despite having access to sensitive business operations via 11 MCP tools. The highest-priority remediation items are establishing a user authentication system, hardening the JWT infrastructure with JWKS and key rotation, adding Valkey authentication, and implementing security-specific alerting.
